title: Coconut and lime traybake
subtitle: A moist desiccated coconut sponge with a lime drizzle
excerpt: A simple tray-baked coconut sponge soaked while still hot with a sharp lime juice and sugar drizzle. Tender, fragrant, and keeps well for three days. Cuts into fifteen pieces.
sourceNotes: Traybakes are a British domestic baking form popularised in the second half of the twentieth century. The combination of desiccated coconut with citrus drizzle is widely documented in British home-baking guides from the 1970s onwards. No single public-domain text records this exact combination. The recipe draws on the standard traybake method with the drizzle-soaking technique documented across British household baking literature.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze the undrizzled traybake. Drizzle after thawing.
recipe.batchNotes: Doubles cleanly into a large deep roasting tin.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: The traybake keeps in a tin for 3 days. The lime flavour deepens on day two.

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: fan oven; 190°C in a conventional oven

glossaryTerms.definition: A cake baked in a shallow rectangular or square tin in a single layer rather than round tins. The large surface area means a shorter bake time than a deep round cake. Traybakes are cut into squares or rectangles to serve. The shallow depth means the batter cooks evenly without the hot outer edges outrunning a raw centre.

content.text: is the simplest form of cake. One bowl, one tin, no layers to level and sandwich. The desiccated coconut keeps this one moist for days; the lime drizzle applied hot is the other reason it stays good. The lime zest in the batter fades slightly in the oven, which is why the drizzle matters.

Preheat the oven to 180°C fan. Grease and line a 30 × 20 cm baking tin with baking paper. Beat the softened {{unsalted-butter}} and {{caster-sugar}} (cake amount) until pale and fluffy. Beat in the {{eggs}} one at a time. Fold in the {{self-raising-flour}}, {{desiccated-coconut}} (cake amount), {{lime}} zest, and {{whole-milk}}. Spread into the prepared tin and level the surface.

Bake for 23–25 minutes, until well-risen, golden, and a skewer inserted into the centre comes out clean. While the cake bakes, stir together the {{lime}} juice and {{caster-sugar}} (drizzle amount). The moment the cake comes out of the oven, poke holes across the surface with a skewer and pour over the drizzle. Scatter with {{desiccated-coconut}} if using. Leave in the tin until completely cool, then cut into squares.
